entries.summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle, stagflation-adjacent regime where the Fed is pinned (28% June cut probability), the 10-year sits at 4.38%, and equities trade at 20.9x forward earnings with a negative equity risk premium — a backdrop hostile to multiple expansion and duration risk. The five picks express this view through three distinct alpha vectors: (1) AI infrastructure compounders with secular earnings momentum that can grow through macro headwinds (GOOGL, AVGO), (2) a volatility/capital-markets beneficiary that monetizes the elevated-rate-and-geopolitical environment directly (GS), (3) a defensive-growth secular compounder insulated from tariff and energy shocks (LLY), and (4) a hard-asset hedge providing negative correlation to the AI equity sleeve in a risk-off scenario (GLD). All five names carry over from prior positioning — GOOGL, AVGO, GS, LLY, and GLD — with thesis continuity confirmed by fresh Q1 2026 earnings beats across GOOGL (94% EPS upside), AVGO (106% AI revenue growth), and GS ($17.55 EPS vs. $16.47 estimate); no thesis breaks have occurred. The dominant risk being underwritten is an AI capex cycle stall or a sovereign debt shock that compresses the AI infrastructure multiple sharply — GLD and LLY serve as the structural hedges against that scenario.
entries.diversificationNote: The five picks span four distinct GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVGO), Financials (GS), Health Care (LLY), and Commodities/Real Assets (GLD) — well within the 2-per-sector hard constraint. The accepted factor concentration is that GOOGL and AVGO both have AI infrastructure exposure, but at complementary points in the value chain (cloud application layer vs. custom silicon/networking), limiting single-factor correlation risk.

entries.summary: The regime call is unchanged: late-cycle stagflation-adjacent, with the Fed pinned at 3.5–3.75%, re-accelerating inflation (core PCE 3.2%), and equities trading at a negative equity risk premium (3.82% earnings yield vs. 4.38% 10Y). The portfolio expresses this in four deliberate layers: (1) AVGO and GOOGL as the two highest-conviction AI infrastructure compounders where fundamentals are genuinely accelerating into and past current valuations — AVGO via custom silicon with $100B in 2027 AI revenue visibility, GOOGL via Cloud re-acceleration at 63% growth and an undemanding 26x forward P/E; (2) GS as the capital markets quality franchise that benefits directly from volatility, steepening curve, and M&A recovery — Druckenmiller's XLF macro rotation bet; (3) LLY as the recession-resistant secular growth name in healthcare with GLP-1 demand durability and pipeline optionality; (4) GLD as the mandatory defensive sleeve with a defensiveScore of 4, providing stagflation hedge, Iran war premium protection, and near-zero equity correlation. AVGO, GS, and LLY are direct carry-overs from the prior 5-pick book with theses intact — GOOGL replaces ANET (which drew down 9.2% post-earnings on gross margin concerns) and GLD is maintained as the defensive anchor replacing IEF given the superior stagflation hedge profile. The dominant risk underwritten is an AI capex cycle reversal where a hyperscaler cuts 2026 capex guidance, which would simultaneously impair AVGO and GOOGL — the portfolio's largest two weights — though their different points in the value chain (custom silicon vs. Cloud platform) provide partial offset.
entries.diversificationNote: The five picks span four distinct sectors: Information Technology (AVGO), Communication Services (GOOGL), Financials (GS), Health Care (LLY), and Commodities/Real Assets (GLD) — satisfying the hard 3-sector minimum with a four-sector spread. AVGO and GOOGL share AI infrastructure spending as a common tailwind, but represent structurally different revenue models (fabless semiconductor vs. cloud platform/advertising), different customer bases, and different sensitivity to any single hyperscaler decision — an accepted and consciously sized concentration at 53% combined weight given the strength of the AI capex cycle evidence.

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF

entries.summary: The macro regime remains late-cycle stagflation-adjacent — Fed on hold at 3.5-3.75%, core PCE at 3.2%, real GDP at 2.0% SAAR, and the Warsh confirmation vote imminent — but today's most important development is an intraday regime signal: oil briefly broke below $100 on Iran ceasefire hopes, Energy is down -3.26% as of this morning (the worst sector), and Financials are rotating to +1.04% leadership. The growth book maintains the high-conviction GOOGL, AVGO, GS, LLY quartet from prior days — all theses intact with strong Q1 earnings beats and positive estimate revisions — while adding AMZN (the cleanest hyperscaler momentum compounder at a post-earnings reset), MSFT (supply-constrained Azure demand), XOM (best-in-class energy value at discounted entry post-ceasefire selloff), VST (AI power demand compounder reporting tomorrow), RTX (defense secular spend), and SPGI (Financials rotation quality compounder). The defensive sleeve spans gold (GLD — stagflation/geopolitical hedge), intermediate treasuries (IEF — equity drawdown hedge with 4.3% carry), dividend equity (SCHD — income ballast), Berkshire (BRK.B — record cash optionality), and NextEra (NEE — regulated utility + AI power growth). The dominant risk underwritten is an AI capex bubble deflation where enterprise AI ROI fails to materialize in H2 2026, simultaneously pressuring GOOGL Cloud, AVGO ASICs, MSFT Azure, and AMZN AWS guidance — a scenario partially hedged by the 22% defensive sleeve weighting and GLD's non-correlation to tech multiples.
entries.diversificationNote: The growth book spans six GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVGO, MSFT), Financials (GS, SPGI), Health Care (LLY), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), Energy (XOM), Utilities (VST), and Industrials (RTX) — well within the 3-per-sector and 5-sector minimum constraints; AVGO and MSFT share AI infrastructure exposure but operate at complementary points in the value chain (custom ASICs vs. cloud platform). The defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: gold commodity (GLD), intermediate-duration US Treasuries (IEF), broad dividend equity (SCHD), and regulated utility with growth (NEE), plus the fortress conglomerate (BRK.B) — explicitly avoiding a double-up in any single defensive category.

entries.summary: The portfolio expresses a late-cycle, stagflation-adjacent regime thesis where the Fed is pinned at 3.5-3.75% with core PCE at 3.2%, the equity risk premium is near zero (earnings yield 3.82% vs 10y at 4.38%), and capital is rotating toward quality, pricing power, and real assets. The growth book concentrates on two structural secular themes — AI infrastructure (GOOGL, AVGO, MSFT, AMZN) and inflation/cycle beneficiaries (XOM, RTX, VST, GS, SPGI) — with LLY as the 'super-defensive growth' anchor that straddles both sleeves in character. Key carry-overs from prior days include GOOGL (#1, thesis intact post-earnings validation), AVGO (#2, June 3 earnings catalyst approaching), GS (#3 carry, Druckenmiller XLF macro backing), LLY (#4, raised guidance intact), and AMZN (re-added from May 6 book with Ackman's 65% position increase as conviction support); XOM replaces pure-energy ETF exposure with a name-level entry on today's 4% pullback. The dominant risk being underwritten is AI capex ROI disappointment — if hyperscalers signal a pause in data center spend, GOOGL, AVGO, MSFT, and AMZN reprice simultaneously, which is partially offset by the defensive sleeve's gold, dividend-income, and low-beta pharma/staples positioning.
entries.diversificationNote: The growth book spans 5 GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVGO, MSFT), Health Care (LLY), Financials (GS, SPGI), Energy (XOM), Industrials (RTX), Utilities (VST), and Consumer Discretionary (AMZN) — no sector exceeds 3 names; the AI infrastructure theme is expressed across GOOGL+AVGO+MSFT+AMZN but at different value-chain points (search/cloud, custom silicon, enterprise software, cloud infrastructure) reducing co-movement risk. The defensive sleeve spans 4 distinct exposures: gold (GLD), broad dividend quality income (SCHD), materials/industrial compounder (LIN), pharma defensive (ABBV), and consumer staples (KO) — deliberately omitting long-duration Treasuries (TLT/IEF) given the stagflation-adjacent regime makes duration unattractive.

entries.summary: The portfolio operates in a late-cycle stagflationary regime — real policy rates mildly restrictive at 3.50–3.75% Fed funds vs. 3.0% core PCE, record S&P margins at 13.4% and 84% Q1 beat rate justify a Cautiously Bullish posture, but the 20.9x forward P/E and re-accelerating energy inflation (12.5% YoY energy CPI) leave zero margin for error. The 10-name growth book concentrates in the three highest-conviction alpha sources of this regime: AI infrastructure monetizers (GOOGL, AVGO, META, AMZN, ANET) with genuine revenue acceleration, quality compounders with pricing power independent of the AI cycle (LLY), and direct inflation/war beneficiaries (XOM, GS, RTX, SPGI). Key carry-overs from the past week are GOOGL, AVGO, GS, LLY, XOM, RTX, and SPGI — theses fully intact with no triggering events; MSFT was rotated out (the momentum screen flagged -20% 6-month relative performance) and replaced with ANET (clean extended flag, direct AI capex beneficiary with differentiated network-layer exposure); META and AMZN added to complete the AI monetization triad. The dominant risk being underwritten is AI capex disappointment — if one or more hyperscalers signals ROI concerns, the GOOGL/AVGO/AMZN/ANET cluster faces simultaneous multiple compression; the 21% combined defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, IEF, BRK.B, VST) provides the convex hedge.
entries.diversificationNote: Growth book spans 5 GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL, META — 2 names), Information Technology (AVGO, ANET — 2 names), Financials (GS, SPGI — 2 names), Health Care (LLY — 1 name), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — 1 name), Industrials (RTX — 1 name), and Energy (XOM — 1 name); the two Communication Services names sell into entirely different end markets (digital advertising vs. cloud/search AI) and the two IT names occupy distinct value-chain positions (custom ASIC silicon vs. data center Ethernet switching). The defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: physical gold inflation/geopolitical hedge (GLD), broad dividend quality equity income (SCHD), intermediate Treasury duration/recession hedge (IEF), and fortress conglomerate/cash optionality (BRK.B), with VST providing a utilities AI-power demand bridge — no two defensive positions share the same primary return driver.

entries.summary: The portfolio is constructed for a late-cycle stagflation overlay regime: the Fed on hold at 3.50–3.75% with core PCE at 3.1%, April CPI printing tomorrow (May 12) as the dominant near-term event risk, and Kevin Warsh assuming the Fed chair on May 15 in an environment that structurally blocks rate cuts. The growth book overweights AI infrastructure (GOOGL, AVGO, AMZN, ANET), defense/geopolitical beneficiaries (RTX, XOM), financials (GS, SPGI) benefiting from yield curve steepening, and secular defensible growth (LLY, VST) — all sectors explicitly identified as 'what's working' in the macro briefing. The defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, IEF, BRK.B, KO) spans gold, dividend equity, intermediate duration, a cash-fortress compounder, and a staples brand, providing genuine non-correlation in a drawdown. All 10 growth names are carry-overs from prior sessions (GOOGL, AVGO, GS, LLY, AMZN, RTX, VST, SPGI, XOM daily carries; ANET added May 8), with no thesis breaks identified — the dominant risk being underwritten is a hotter-than-expected April CPI print that triggers a broad equity de-rating before the NVDA earnings (May 20) and Warsh inaugural speech create the next major catalyst window.
entries.diversificationNote: The growth book spans 6 distinct GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVGO, ANET — two AI infrastructure names with complementary value-chain exposure: custom ASICs vs. AI networking), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), Industrials (RTX), Health Care (LLY), Financials (GS, SPGI — two quality financial names with different revenue drivers: trading/IB vs. data/ratings), Energy (XOM), and Utilities (VST); the defensive sleeve spans gold (GLD), broad dividend equity (SCHD), intermediate-duration Treasuries (IEF), diversified insurance/value compounder (BRK.B), and consumer staples brand (KO) — with no single exposure double-counted.

entries.summary: The macro regime is late-cycle stagflation with an Iran war energy shock overlay — April CPI printed 3.8% YoY (hottest since May 2023), core CPI hit 0.4% MoM, rate hike odds are now at 30% per CME, and WTI crude swung between $88–$107 last week with Trump reportedly considering restarting combat operations. The 10-name growth book expresses this via three structural AI infrastructure names (GOOGL, AVGO, AMZN) that are differentiated across the value chain (cloud+search, custom silicon, hyperscaler cloud), two direct Iran-regime beneficiaries (XOM in Energy Tier 1, RTX in Defense/Industrials), two financials that benefit from rate volatility and trading revenue (GS, SPGI), one healthcare secular compounder (LLY), one AI power merchant (VST), and one catch-up AI/ad-tech play (META). Core carryovers from the prior week — GOOGL, AVGO, LLY, GS, AMZN, RTX, XOM, VST, SPGI — all retain intact theses reinforced by today's CPI; META is elevated from a medium-conviction hold to a ranked growth position given its fundamental/price momentum divergence creating the most attractive risk/reward in Communication Services. The defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) spans gold, dividend equity, a cash-fortress conglomerate, intermediate Treasuries as tail-risk insurance, and a consumer staples quality compounder — the dominant risk being underwritten is that a Warsh hawkish pivot or Iran re-escalation reprices growth multiples and forces a defensive rotation the sleeve is designed to partially cushion.
entries.diversificationNote: The growth book spans 6 distinct GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL, META — two names sharing the ad/AI theme but with different revenue models), Information Technology (AVGO), Health Care (LLY), Energy (XOM), Industrials (RTX), Financials (GS, SPGI — two names accepted given the sector's Tier 1 ranking and their differentiated catalysts: trading revenue vs. ratings/data analytics), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), and Utilities (VST); GOOGL and AVGO share AI infrastructure secular exposure but operate at entirely different points in the value chain (cloud software monetization vs. custom ASIC silicon). The defensive sleeve spans five distinct exposures — gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality equity (SCHD), conglomerate/cash fortress (BRK.B), intermediate Treasuries as tail-risk insurance (IEF), and consumer staples quality compounder (KO) — with no duplicate exposure.
